coredata.dc:title: Achieving ultrasensitive detection of glyphosate in fruits and vegetables using a triple-mode strategy based on carbon dots nanozymes derived from expired drugs
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Wei
Cheng, Hui-Ling
Huang, Yan-Li
Ni, Chen
Shi, Yu-han
Xu, Zhi-Hong
Zhang, Guo-Qi
coredata.dc:description: Rapid and sensitive detection of glyphosate (GLP) holds significant importance in the monitoring of environmental pollution and potential risks to human health. In this study, carbon dots nanozymes (CDszymes) with peroxidase-like activity were synthesized rapidly using a microwave-assisted method, employing expired drugs as raw materials. In the presence of H2O2, CDszymes catalyze the oxidation of TMB to generate blue oxTMB, which exhibits a photothermal effect under near-infrared light irradiation; an inner filter effect (IFE) may occur between oxTMB and CDszymes. By coupling the cascade catalysis of AChE and ChOx to generate H2O2, GLP effectively inhibits the activity of AChE, constructing a colorimetric/fluorescent/photothermal response platform for GLP. In colorimetry, the detection limit of GLP was 0.33 ng/mL. The detection limits of GLP by fluorescence method and photothermal method were 0.02 ng/mL and 0.41 ng/mL, respectively. The efficacy of this methodology has been successfully demonstrated in fruit and vegetable, it also provides a strategy for the high-value conversion of expired drugs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots nanozymes
Peroxidase-like activity
Glyphosate
Colorimetric
Photothermal
Fluorescence

Real sample detection of fruits and vegetables
POD performance and mechanism of CDszymes
Detection of GLP in fruits and vegetables

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We appreciate the financial support from the Xihua University Science and Technology Innovation Competition Project for Postgraduate Students (No. YK20240181 and YK20240192). We are grateful for the financial support of Sichuan Students\u2019 Platform for innovation and entrepreneurship training program (No. S202310623083). We would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the XPS and TEM testing and analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Synthesis process of CDszymes. (B) Fluorescence spectra of CDszymes at different excitation wavelengths. (C) TEM image of CDszymes. (D) FT-IR spectra of CDszymes, acetaminophen, and PEI. (E) XPS spectra of CDszymes. (F) High-resolution spectra of C(1s), (G) N(1s), and (H) O(1s).

(A) Comparison of peroxidase activities among different samples, all at a concentration of 2.0 mg/mL. (B) EPR spectra detection of ·OH in H2O2 and CDszymes + H2O2. Steady-state kinetics of CDszymes at different concentrations of (C) TMB and (D) H2O2 analyzed using Lineweaver-Burk plots. Error bars represent the standard deviation of three independent measurements. (E) UV–visible absorption spectra of different reaction systems with TMB as the substrate. (F) Fluorescence quenching spectra of CDszymes and CDszymes + oxTMB.

(A) UV–visible absorption spectra of GLP in different reaction systems. (B) Fluorescence spectra of GLP in different reaction systems. (C) Temperature variations of GLP in different reaction systems (1: CDszymes; 2: CDszymes + TMB; 3: CDszymes + TMB + ACh + AChE; 4: CDszymes + TMB + ACh + AChE + ChOx; 5: CDszymes + TMB + ACh + AChE + ChOx + GLP).

(A) Absorption spectra of reaction systems containing different concentrations of GLP. (B) Relationship between GLP concentration and absorbance at 652 nm. Inset: Calibration curve for GLP detection. (C) Fluorescence spectra of reaction systems containing different concentrations of GLP. (D) Relationship between GLP concentration and fluorescence intensity. Inset: Calibration curve for GLP detection. (E) Relationship between reaction systems containing different concentrations of GLP and temperature. (F) Colorimetric, (G) Fluorescent, and (H) Photothermal detection of potential interferents in fruits and vegetables. All sample concentrations are 5 μg/mL. Insets: Corresponding digital images. Each error bar represents the standard deviation of three independent measurements.
Illustration of the colorimetric, fluorescence and photothermal third-mode for GLP detection.

head.ce:title: Achieving ultrasensitive detection of glyphosate in fruits and vegetables using a triple-mode strategy based on carbon dots nanozymes derived from expired drugs

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemisty, School of Science, Xihua University, Chengdu 610039, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemisty, School of Science, Xihua University, Chengdu 610039, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Asymmetric Synthesis and Chiral Technology Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, Xihua University, Chengdu 610039, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Asymmetric Synthesis and Chiral Technology Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Asymmetric Synthesis and Chiral Technology Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, Xihua University, Chengdu 610039, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemisty, School of Science, Xihua University, Chengdu 610039, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rapid and sensitive detection of glyphosate (GLP) holds significant importance in the monitoring of environmental pollution and potential risks to human health. In this study, carbon dots nanozymes (CDszymes) with peroxidase-like activity were synthesized rapidly using a microwave-assisted method, employing expired drugs as raw materials. In the presence of H2O2, CDszymes catalyze the oxidation of TMB to generate blue oxTMB, which exhibits a photothermal effect under near-infrared light irradiation; an inner filter effect (IFE) may occur between oxTMB and CDszymes. By coupling the cascade catalysis of AChE and ChOx to generate H2O2, GLP effectively inhibits the activity of AChE, constructing a colorimetric/fluorescent/photothermal response platform for GLP. In colorimetry, the detection limit of GLP was 0.33 ng/mL. The detection limits of GLP by fluorescence method and photothermal method were 0.02 ng/mL and 0.41 ng/mL, respectively. The efficacy of this methodology has been successfully demonstrated in fruit and vegetable, it also provides a strategy for the high-value conversion of expired drugs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Glyphosate(GLP) has been widely used in global agricultural production due to its high efficiency, broad spectrum, low toxicity and easy decomposition [1]. The European Union has reduced the maximum residue limit for glyphosate in 153 agricultural products, including tea, from 0.1 mg/kg to 0.05 mg/kg. In China, the limit for fruits like apples is also set at 0.05 mg/kg. Consequently, monitoring glyphosate levels in crops, fruits, and vegetables is increasingly crucial [2]. However, excessive residue of GLP may lead to various health issues, such as Alzheimer's disease, depression, endocrine disruption, and cancers [3–5]. Several quantitative methods have been employed to determine GLP, including gas chromatography [6], high-performance liquid chromatography [7], ion exchange chromatography [8], chromatography-mass spectrometry [9], and sheathless capillary electrophoresis-mass spectrometry [10]. However, most of these methods require expensive equipment, complex sample preparation, and skilled technicians. Moreover, they are not suitable for on-site testing. These drawbacks severely limit their application in rapid on-site screening, prompting efforts to explore low-cost, easy-to-handle, and rapid readout GLP sensing platforms.

Numerous convenient and cost-effective methods have been developed for GLP determination, yet the majority are single-mode assays. These single-mode methods offer limited information and are susceptible to external interference, potentially leading to false signals. Conversely, dual-mode methods enhance information diversity and mitigate such interference [11–13]. Furthermore, the outputs from dual-mode methods can cross-validate each other, thereby markedly enhancing the accuracy and reliability of the results. However, discrepancies among dual-mode results pose challenges in determining the definitive outcome, highlighting the critical need for the development of triple-mode methods. To our knowledge, the utilization of triple-mode assays for GLP detection remains relatively constrained. For instance, Luo et al. [14] developed a triple-signal sensing approach integrating fluorescence, colorimetry, and photothermal signals for GLP detection, thereby introducing a novel avenue for multifaceted sensing methodologies.

Acetaminophen is a commonly used medication during COVID-19. The bulk purchase of acetaminophen may lead to the accumulation of expired drugs. Improper disposal of these expired drugs, such as throwing them away in the trash or flushing them down the drain, can result in their entry into soil and water, contaminating the environment [15,16]. Certain professional organizations manage the incineration or chemical treatment of expired drugs, which can contribute to air pollution and greenhouse gas emissions, thereby adversely affecting climate change. Therefore, there is a pressing need for advanced conversion technologies to derive high-value products from expired drugs.

Carbon dots nanozymes (CDszymes) represent a novel class of nanomaterials characterized by both optical properties and catalytic activities akin to natural enzymes. Due to its cost-effectiveness, enhanced stability, and straightforward synthesis compared to natural enzymes, carbon dots nanozymes have garnered considerable attention [17–19]. CDszymes exhibiting peroxidase-like (POD) activity are utilized in chemical sensing applications. Jiang et al. [20]. synthesized CuCeTA nano-flowers exhibiting exceptional POD activity for GLP detection. This was achieved through the assembly of tannic acid, copper sulfate pentahydrate, and cerium(III) nitrate hexahydrate, achieving an impressive detection limit as low as 0.025 ppm. Furthermore, doping metals into carbon dots (CDs) represents a straightforward and effective strategy to achieve superior catalytic activity. For instance, Yang et al. [21] synthesized iron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (Fe/N-CDs) with POD activity, showcasing enhanced catalytic efficacy surpassing that of horseradish peroxidase (HRP).

Herein, using expired acetaminophen as the starting material, CDszymes with POD activity were synthesized rapidly via microwave-assisted method. Furthermore, a self-calibrating colorimetric/fluorescent/photothermal triple-mode sensing platform for GLP detection was developed utilizing the POD activity of CDszymes (Scheme 1 ). The sensing mechanism entails the enzymatic reaction of acetylcholine (ACh) with acetylcholinesterase (AChE), resulting in the production of choline. Subsequently, choline oxidase (ChOx) catalyzes the conversion of choline to hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). CDszymes further catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 into hydroxyl radicals (•OH), which then oxidize 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to form a blue product (oxTMB). This process exhibits significant color change, and the generated oxTMB exhibits notable photothermal characteristics under near-infrared light, leading to fluorescence quenching of CDszymes due to inner filter effect (IFE). A three-enzyme cascade system comprising AChE, ChOx, and CDszymes with specific POD activity was employed for the detection of the organophosphorus pesticide GLP.
Reagents and instruments can be found in the support information.

Under optimized conditions (Fig. S1), CDszymes were synthesized as follows: 0.3 g of acetaminophen, 0.6 g of polyethyleneimine (PEI), and 1.5 mM FeCl3·H2O were dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water. The solution was transferred to a 30 mL glass reaction tube, heated at 160 °C for 5 min using a microwave synthesizer (Power: 850 W), and subsequently centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min. The resulting supernatant was collected and stored at 4 °C in a refrigerator for further use. In the preparation of CDszymes, if polyethyleneimine (PEI) is not added, the remaining steps are identical to those described above and are designated as CDszymes-1. If acetaminophen is not included, the remaining steps follow the same procedure as outlined above and are designated as CDszymes-2. Additionally, if the synthesis process does not involve the incorporation of doped metal iron, it is designated as CDszymes-3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of POD activity of CDszymes
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the POD-like catalytic activity of CDszymes, a test tube containing 1 mM acetate buffer (pH 4.0) was prepared. Subsequently, 200 μL of 2 mg/mL CDszymes, 200 μL of 5 mM H2O2, and 200 μL of 6 mM TMB were thoroughly mixed and incubated at 25 °C for 6 min. The absorbance at 652 nm (A652) was measured using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer to quantify peroxidase activity.

The electron spin resonance (ESR) experiment was conducted at room temperature to further confirm the generation of hydroxyl radicals (·OH) using 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as the spin trapping agent. To measure ·OH, 50 μL of CDszymes solution (2 mg/mL) and 50 μL of H2O2 (5 mM) were added to 200 μL of 0.2 M acetate buffer (pH 4.0). Immediately after, 20 μL of DMPO was added to the above mixture. Subsequently, the resulting solution was extracted using a quartz capillary and transferred to a glass tube for ESR detection.

Typically, 200 μL of 2 mg/mL CDszymes is mixed with 200 µL of H2O2 in 1 mL of HAc-NaAc buffer solution (pH 4.0). Then, 200 μL of 6 mM TMB is added to these systems, and a colorimetric reaction is carried out at 25°C for 6 min. Finally, UV–visible absorption spectra (absorption wavelength at 652 nm) and fluorescence spectra (Ex/Em = 400 nm/515 nm, with excitation and emission slit widths of 5 nm) are recorded. To record the photothermal signal, after irradiation of the catalytic products with a 660 nm laser (1 W/cm2) for 15 min, the temperature change (ΔT) of the reaction system is read using an infrared imaging device and used to establish a linear relationship with H2O2 concentration.

Different concentrations of 200 μL volume of GLP were incubated with 200 μL of 25 U/mL AChE solution at 37 °C for 10 min. Then, after adding 200 μL of 15 U /mL ChOx solution and incubating for 30 min with 30 μL of 5 mM ACh, 200 μL of 2 mg/mL CDszymes, 200 μL of 6 mM TMB, and 1.0 mL of 0.2 M pH 4.0 HAc-NaAc buffer solution were added for another incubation at 25°C for 6 min. UV–vis absorption spectra (absorption wavelength at 652 nm) and fluorescence spectra (with excitation wavelength/emission wavelength of 400/515 nm; excitation and emission slit widths of 5 nm) were recorded. After irradiation of the catalytic products with a 660 nm laser (1 W/cm2) for 15 min, the temperature of the solution was recorded using an infrared imaging device.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real sample detection of fruits and vegetables
ce:section.ce:para: 10 g of locally sourced samples (apples, strawberries, cucumbers, and tomatoes) purchased from the local market were crushed and soaked in 20 mL of acetone for 24 h. Subsequently, the supernatant was extracted and filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to remove insoluble substances. The filtrate was then mixed with different concentrations of GLP (20, 60, 100 ng/mL) and diluted 20-fold. Using this method, tri-modal analysis including colorimetry, fluorescence, and photothermal methods was performed on GLP.

CDszymes were synthesized using a microwave-assisted methodology (Fig. 1 A). Fig. 1B illustrates the fluorescence properties of CDszymes, showing maximum excitation/emission wavelengths at 380 nm/515 nm. Fig. S2 A shows CDSzyms-1 and CDSzyms-2 with little fluorescence. Using N-(4-hydroxyphenyl) acetamide as the carbon source, the influence of pharmaceutical excipients on CDszymes synthesis was investigated. As illustrated in Fig. S3, the maximum fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of both CDszymes remained consistent, suggesting minimal influence from other components present in acetaminophen. The morphology and microstructure of the synthesized CDszymes were investigated using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) for detailed nanomaterial characterization [22,23]. As shown in Fig. 1C, the synthesized CDszymes exhibited highly dispersed quasi-spherical structures. The particle size distribution of CDszymes ranged from 1.6 to 4.5 nm, with a mean diameter of approximately 2.5 nm. The surface functional groups of CDszymes were characterized using Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy [24] (FT-IR) (Fig. 1D) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy [25–27] (XPS) (Fig. 1E). The absorption peak at 3460 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of OH/NH bonds, while the peak at 1565 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of CO bonds. Additionally, the peak at 1487 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of CC/CN bonds. XPS spectra of CDszymes showed major elements C, O, N, and Fe, with corresponding contents of 76.02 %, 14.26 %, 9.60 %, and 0.12 %, respectively. The high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 1F) exhibited three peaks at binding energies of 284.8 eV, 285.9 eV, and 287.8 eV, corresponding to CC/CC, CN/CO, and C-O bonds, respectively. The high-resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. 1G) displayed two prominent peaks at 399.7 eV and 401.4 eV, corresponding to C-N and NH bonds, respectively. In the high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 1H), a peak observed at 532.8 eV signifies CO bonds. The XPS results were consistent with FT-IR findings, confirming the presence of hydroxyl, carbonyl, and amide groups in the synthesized CDszymes. These electronic donor and acceptor groups facilitate intra-molecular charge transfer effects, contributing to their catalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: POD performance and mechanism of CDszymes
ce:section.ce:para: Utilizing the chromogenic substrate TMB to probe the POD characteristics of CDszymes. As shown in Fig. 2A, when CDszymes-3 were added to the TMB solution, no color reaction occurred in the presence or absence of H2O2, indicating that CDszymes-3 did not possess peroxidase or oxidase activity. However, upon addition of CDszymes, colorless TMB was oxidized to blue TMB in the presence of H2O2, indicating the POD activity of CDszymes. Fig. S2B shows that POD activity of CDszyms-1 and CDszyms-2 is not as high as that of CDszymes. Additionally, for another common peroxidase substrate, ortho-phenylenediamine (OPD), under the same conditions, a characteristic peak appeared at 450 nm in the UV–visible absorption spectrum of the colorless OPD/H2O2 system after reacting with CDszymes (Fig. S4), further confirming the POD properties of CDszymes. It is worth mentioning that CDszymes synthesized under the same conditions using N-(4-hydroxyphenyl)acetamide (the main component) showed similar POD activity to CDszymes (Fig. S5), indicating that expired acetaminophen can be valorized. Fig. S6 shows the effect of different metals on CDszymes, and the results demonstrate that the catalytic activity is highest when iron ions are doped, thus subsequent studies will employ iron-doped CDszymes. To further elucidate the catalytic mechanism of CDszymes, the generation of hydroxyl radicals (•OH) was directly confirmed using EPR [28]. The EPR signal of •OH can be clearly observed (Fig. 2B). Therefore, we propose the reaction mechanism: under the catalysis of Fe atoms, CDszymes in the system generate •OH in the presence of H2O2, oxidizing colorless TMB to blue oxTMB. Furthermore, the activity of CDszymes showed no significant decrease after 20 days (Fig. S7), indicating good stability, which will facilitate their further application.

Steady-state kinetic studies of the POD catalytic performance of CDszymes were conducted by monitoring CDszymes at different concentrations of TMB and H2O2, including the maximum reaction rate (V max) and the Michaelis constant (K m) [29] (Fig. 2C, D). The K m values of CDszymes for TMB and H2O2 were 0.07 and 0.03 mM, respectively, which are lower than the K m values of other nanozymes (Table S1), indicating that CDszymes have better affinity for TMB and H2O2. Meanwhile, the corresponding V max values for TMB and H2O2 were 66.4 × 10−5 and 537.6 × 10−5 M/s, respectively, which are higher than those of most other nanozymes, suggesting that CDszymes have a rapid reaction rate.

It is worth noting that the intrinsic fluorescence of CDszymes is not affected by TMB itself, but is significantly quenched after co-incubation (Fig. 2E), indicating that the oxidation product oxTMB is the cause of fluorescence quenching. To determine the quenching mechanism [30], the fluorescence lifetimes of CDszymes and CDszymes + oxTMB were compared. As shown in Fig. 2F, the fluorescence lifetime of CDszymes did not change significantly upon addition of oxTMB. Therefore, considering the broad absorption peak of oxTMB near 652 nm overlapping with the emission peak of CDszymes at 515 nm, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of CDszymes can be attributed to the inner filter effect (IFE) between oxTMB and CDszymes.

The dual-mode sensing strategy allows for mutual calibration of detection results, enhancing accuracy and reliability. In cases of inconsistency, introducing a third sensing strategy can be effective in more complex scenarios. Therefore, in this study, a self-calibrating trimodal sensing platform (Scheme 1) for detecting GLP using CDszymes was constructed. GLP had no significant effect on the POD activity of CDszymes. However, the phosphate group in GLP can establish a stable and robust phosphate ester bond with the serine hydroxyl group of AChE, resulting in an irreversible covalent modification that alters the spatial conformation of AChE and leads to a loss of its enzymatic activity [12,31,32]. This mechanism effectively inhibits the AChE-ChOx catalyzed production of H2O2. Consequently, in this study, H2O2 was first detected, and then GLP was indirectly detected.

First, the feasibility of colorimetric, fluorescent, and photothermal methods for the determination of H2O2 was studied. This was because CDszymes can catalyze the oxidation of TMB by H2O2 to produce a blue product (oxTMB); oxTMB exhibits photothermal properties under near-infrared (NIR) irradiation; the inner filter effect (IFE) between oxTMB and CDszymes leads to the quenching of CDszymes fluorescence. Absorbance at 652 nm was only observed in the presence of CDszymes/TMB/H2O2 (Fig. S8A). Similarly, fluorescence quenching of CDszymes at 515 nm was observed in the presence of oxTMB (Fig. S9A). Fig. S10A shows that a temperature change was only induced by the generation of oxTMB when CDszymes/TMB/H2O2 were present simultaneously. Given the impact of environmental factors such as pH, nanozyme concentration, and substrate concentrations on the catalytic activity of CDszymes, the detection conditions were systematically optimized (Fig. S11). H2O2 was determined under optimal conditions: the linear range of the colorimetric method was 0.2–2 mM (LOD = 0.07 mM; 3 S/N) (Fig. S8B, C); the linear range of the fluorescent method was 0.08–1 mM (LOD = 0.01 mM) (Fig. S9B, C); and the linear range of the photothermal method was 0.2–1.0 mM (LOD = 0.13 mM) (Fig. S10B). Therefore, a trimodal sensing platform capable of sensitive detection of H2O2 using three signals was developed.

The feasibility of colorimetric, fluorescent, and photothermal methods for detecting GLP was studied. Fig. 3 A shows that GLP (5 μg/mL), ACh (5 mM), AChE (20 U/mL), and ChOx (10 U/mL) have minimal impact on the CDszymes/TMB system, indicated by the slight changes in A652 and temperature. However, upon addition of GLP, A652 and temperature of the ACh/AChE/ChOx/CDszymes/TMB system significantly decrease. Due to the inner filter effect between oxTMB and CDszymes, which leads to fluorescence quenching of CDszymes, the addition of GLP leads to a reduction in oxTMB production by lowering AChE activity. This subsequently restores CDszymes' fluorescence and mitigates the thermal effect of oxTMB under near-infrared light. Consequently, Fig. 3(B, C) demonstrate that upon addition of GLP, the fluorescence intensity of the ACh/AChE/ChOx/CDszymes/TMB system markedly increases while temperature decreases. Therefore, CDszymes can establish a platform for colorimetric, fluorescent, and photothermal detection of GLP.

Under optimal conditions, the methodological performance of the platform for detecting GLP was investigated. Fig. 4 (A, B) depict the UV absorbance spectra of GLP within different concentration ranges. The detection limit of GLP was 0.33 ng/mL within a linear range of 0.5–200 ng/mL. Fluorescence spectra of the CDszymes/TMB sensing system at various GLP concentrations are shown in Fig. 4 (C, D), with a linear range of 0.2–200 ng/mL and a detection limit of 0.02 ng/mL for GLP. The temperature changes of the system recorded at different GLP concentrations (Fig. 4E) demonstrated a linear range of 0.5–200 ng/mL, with a detection limit of 0.41 ng/mL for GLP.

Comparing with previous literature, the proposed colorimetric, fluorescent, and photothermal trimodal sensing platform exhibits excellent analytical performance in detecting GLP (Table S2). Furthermore, multimodal sensing can provide multidimensional information, allowing for complementary calibration to enhance accuracy and reliability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of GLP in fruits and vegetables
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the selectivity of the proposed trimodal sensor for GLP detection in real samples, as depicted in Fig. 4(F–H), common interfering ions and substances found in actual samples were selected. These include metal cations (K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+), anions (Cl−, CO3 2−, NO3 −, SO4 2−, HPO4 2−, H2PO4 −), biomolecules (urea, glucose, glycine, glutamic acid, alanine, aspartic acid), and other herbicides (2,4-D-butyl ester, chipton, trifluralin, mesotrione). The concentration of GLP was maintained at 5 ng/mL, while the concentrations of metal ions and biomolecules were set at 100 times that of GLP (5 μg/mL). The results demonstrated no interference from these substances, confirming the excellent selectivity of the sensor for GLP. To validate the practicality of the trimodal sensor, we selected apples, strawberries, cucumbers, and tomatoes as the research objects. As shown in Tables S2-S4, the recovery rates and relative standard deviations of the colorimetric, fluorescent, and photothermal methods were all within acceptable ranges, indicating that the method is suitable for detecting GLP in fruits and vegetables.

Microwave-assisted synthesis of CDszymes using expired acetaminophen as a precursor exhibited excellent stability and catalytic activity. A cascade enzymatic reaction involving AChE, ACh, and ChOx generated H2O2 during the catalytic process, establishing a colorimetric/fluorescent/photothermal trimodal sensing platform for detecting GLP. In fruit and vegetable samples, the recovery rates (n = 3) ranged from 87.0 % to 118.0 %, with a standard deviation of less than 5.1 %, indicating the reliability and practicality of this trimodal sensing platform in real samples. This study provides new insights and methods for the development of efficient, sensitive, and multifunctional sensors, with potential applications in environmental monitoring and food safety.

Wei Wu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Hui-Ling Cheng: Methodology, Investigation. Yan-Li Huang: Methodology, Investigation. Chen Ni: Methodology, Investigation. Yu-han Shi: Methodology, Investigation. Zhi-Hong Xu: Supervision, Conceptualization. Guo-Qi Zhang: Supervision, Visualization, Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We appreciate the financial support from the Xihua University Science and Technology Innovation Competition Project for Postgraduate Students (No. YK20240181 and YK20240192). We are grateful for the financial support of Sichuan Students’ Platform for innovation and entrepreneurship training program (No. S202310623083). We would like to thank Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the XPS and TEM testing and analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.125214.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An efficient chemiluminescence system based on mimic CuMOF/Co3O4 nanoparticles composite for the measurement of glucose and cholesterol
Sens. Actuators B Chem.

Sens. Actuators B Chem.
